text:
The older you get in life, the more precious time becomes. Because as time passes, the more you realize every moment matters—but not in the way we always think or certainly plan. Take traveling, for example. One might reason that planning every minute of an adventure might be the best way to take advantage of the time you have allotted for a vacation. There is a lot to see and do at any tourist destination and not enjoying yourself in every moment seems like wasted or lost time.

But that ideal does not equal reality on a trip. Because the instant your plane lands in paradise, you bump into a new friend, find a cool restaurant you did not expect, or torrential rain cancels your first planned outing. Any one of these spontaneous events, positive or negative, puts your perfectly planned trip agenda out of order. How should you then react? By losing your cool and stressing yourself and others around you by trying to catch up to the plans you made and missed? Or should you go with the flow and simply do and see what you can in the given time?

Through my own experiences, I might opt for the latter. Even if you lose a few minutes, a few hours, or a day due to something unexpected happening, think of it this way: If it is a positive happenstance, like making a new friend or finding a hidden beach, you have a great new memory to take home with you. If it is a negative experience, like an unexpected hurricane or wacky taxi driver, you go home with a funny story to tell. Because the best stories are often the worst experiences for someone else. That is the bottom line - life is what you make of it. And time is precious.

So do not plan too hard and simply enjoy the moment given to you because it is that - a gift.

The Night-owl Paradox: Flexibility, Freedom, and Competing Modes

All humans need sleep. According to the Mayo Clinic, most adults should target seven or more hours of sleep for good health (“Adult Health,” 2025), and even so-called “short sleepers” need four to six hours. The debate is about when those hours should come. Some argue that “early to bed, early to rise” is preferable. Such a schedule allows for consistency and a biorhythm that aligns with the schedule of society. Such a lifestyle is also inflexible and boring. Far superior is the late-night life. Ignoring natural circadian rhythms, this essay argues that the night-owl life offers more flexibility and control, more freedom to toggle on demand between increased excitement and reflective solitude.

Morning people often claim to live more naturally, responsibly, and healthily. They rise early, in synch with the sunrise and birds. Their schedules align with traditional work and education patterns, and, once achieved, this schedule is easy to maintain. This schedule is also rigid, unfree. Morning people tire early, and they wake when the body or the alarm commands, not when they will.

By contrast, the night owl chooses when the day ends. Rising later, they meet the world in full motion and have the energy to persist when others fade. When tasks present, the night owl can stay up to see the work done. There is a reason it is called “pulling an all-nighter”: the night owl is best positioned to expand working hours in response to need.

The benefits of night-owl freedom concern more than just productive capacity, however. Night people also have more freedom to choose between contrasting modes of excitement and reflection. The “night life” is known for high-energy entertainment. Concerts, live shows, and the club all cater to night-time socializers, and every baseball fan knows that the night crowd is more energetic. The morning person struggles to enjoy such scenes; the night owl experiences them with ease. Paradoxically, the late-night hours are also suited for calm reflection. The world slows at night, as many sleep, and solitude comes easy. In the slowness of night, the meditative mind can immerse in reading or music, or the hypnotic dance and crackle of a late-night fire. 

In the end, the night owl has more freedom to choose, more flexibility to do, to enjoy, and to ponder. The morning life has merit, but the night life is best.

The format in which we read a text impacts how we engage with it. Reading books in print is better than reading digitally because it promotes stronger focus, deeper engagement through physical interaction with the text, and more intentional reading.

Print books force focus because they don’t have distractions such as notifications or internet access. When reading, one notification, even if you don’t open it, can pull your attention away from the text and allow for your mind to wander towards other thoughts unrelated to the narrative. It is also very easy to make a quick Google search. This may seem helpful, but in reality will cause the reader to think less critically in the moment. They would rely on the input of others on the Internet to guide them through what could’ve been a more rewarding cognitive exercise had they not outsourced their thinking.

Another reason to prefer reading books in print is the opportunity to hand write annotations directly into the text. Writing notes in the margins or even just underlining a specific word creates a stronger connection with the material. It is a personal and active engagement that helps with comprehension and analysis in a way that highlighting and typing on a screen cannot. Physical annotation creates a real time response and relationship between the reader and the text.

Some argue that not all devices offer distractions with notifications or internet access since there are devices made specifically for reading like Amazon’s Kindle. Digital readers will tell you that this type of device is beneficial to their reading because it allows you to carry up to thousands of books on one small, lightweight device. However, this directly contributes to the inability to focus and deeply engage with one text. Having so many options makes reading less intentional. To deeply focus and engage with literature, one has to be intentional about their practice of reading.

While digital reading may be more convenient for many, those who want to have a serious practice of reading in their life will choose to read in print. Readers will be able to connect more deeply with the narrative of the text when they crack open the spine of a hard copy print book.

Planning a trip in detail is better than traveling spontaneously as you would benefit from cost efficiency, time optimization, and aligned interests with travel companions.

Cost efficiency involves determining which activities are most cost-effective and which restaurants are most affordable. It may be easy to partake in activities or visit restaurants in areas with a high population of tourists; however, there may be less-populated activities and hole-in-the-wall restaurants that provide a better experience for a lower cost that can only be found by planning ahead of time. For example, a souvenir shop in a place with high tourist traffic is bound to sell at higher prices than one that is harder to find from a quick search.

Time optimization involves strategically fitting the most amount of activities and locations to visit in an itinerary rather than constantly scrambling to figure out what to do next. This may include organizing which activities make sense to group within the same day given their locations, planning out meals according to the location of those activities, and incorporating estimated travel times between different locations. Without this preparation, most of the time that could be spent exploring and experiencing activities would be put into making last-minute decisions.

Aligning interests involves making sure that there is something for all travel companions. More spontaneous travel might be difficult for people who are picky with food or activities, and planning everything in detail ensures that these decisions can be made ahead of time rather than in the spur of the moment. If someone in the group has dietary restrictions, it would be in the entire group’s best interest to research restaurants that can accommodate for them, otherwise they may have trouble finding suitable meals.

The best way to enjoy a trip that is the most cost-efficient, optimized for time, and ensures that the interests of all travel companions will be accounted for is to plan it in detail instead of traveling spontaneously.

Spontaneous trips/plans are usually seen as fun however, to make most of your time, taking the time to plan may be better to optimize and enjoy time.

Japan as a travel destination has become increasingly popular travel destination overtime (1). In 2025, Japan saw the most visitors’ arrivals than ever before (2) and recently there has been a 6.4% growth rate from 2025 in tourism (3). However, there is a learning curve to traveling to places like Japan and a lot of times there is different rules for visitors, such as needing to reserve a JR pass prior to arriving to Japan. Without doing so, a tourist may not be able to use the JR train to travel around, limiting the opportunities. For driving in different countries, you might need an international driving permit that can only be obtained while in the United States.  Doing research before and understanding how traveling works inside the city and what is needed for transportation can ensure that the best and quickest option for transport is being used and that options are not just limited to just your arrival city.

For a lot of people, getting the best experience possible while traveling is important and weather can influence in a bad or good way. Showing up to a place in the middle of their rainy season can limit activities and sometimes, transportation. It can also influence the perception of a place. Whether it is too hot that makes standing outside unbearable or too cold that you cannot properly enjoy your travel experience, planning and researching the weather and seasons of a place can help make sure you are taking advantage of everything the place has to offer. It will help ensure that you don’t miss out on activities that you could enjoy in other seasons. In many countries, there are holidays wrapped up in the seasons which can affect accommodation, transportation, restaurants, and activities. Without planning around the weather and/or holidays, a lot of time can be spent trying to figure out solutions.

Different countries and cities have their own rhythm and way of operating. When there is an opportunity to visit somewhere new, it is important to try to enjoy and use your as much as possible and planning your trip, to avoid issues in transportation, weather, and holidays can push you in that direction.

1 https://www.forbes.com/sites/michellegross/2025/07/02/japan-travel-tips-first-time-visitors/
2 https://statistics.jnto.go.jp/en/graph/#graph--inbound--travelers--transition
3 https://statistics.jnto.go.jp/en/graph/

Why You Should Be a Night Owl

The debate over whether it is better to be a “night owl” or an early riser is more than an amusing icebreaker. It embeds a larger argument about the most efficient ways to shape our work and leisure time. Though early risers undoubtedly enjoy some benefits associated with daybreak, night owls follow a schedule that allows them to be more productive and coordinate their leisure hours with “prime time.”

Many people follow a schedule dictated by their working preferences. Indeed, some early risers cite the tranquility of the hours before the traditional workday begins as an ideal time to complete job-related tasks. However, they ultimately structure their day so that their ideal working hours are abruptly interrupted when nine o’clock arrives. In contrast, night owls enjoy a daily structure that provides an uninterrupted period in which they can complete their work. Night owls are never interrupted by a nine-o-clock meeting or ten-o-clock conference call that breaks their train of thought. Instead, they enjoy the productivity gains associated with focused, uninterrupted work.

Night owls also enjoy benefits beyond the confines of their desks. Night owls organize their days so that they are active and energized at “prime time.” They are near their peak engagement level when hottest shows premiere, and, unlike early risers, night owls are never too tired to catch a baseball game on an idyllic summer night. Night owls are not limited to their favorite shows and teams. They can go to the movies, attend concerts, meet friends at bars and clubs, hang out around a bonfire, and discover countless other events that take place in the evening hours. Of course, night owls do miss some leisure activities—especially outdoor activities like hearing the avian “pre-dawn” choir or gathering at the dock before sunrise for a fishing trip—that require alarm clocks to ring before the sun comes up. Yet, night owls may not notice given the other range of activities that are available to them during the evening hours.

The debate about whether it is better to be a night owl or early riser may never be resolved. Early risers gain access to a set of experiences that elude night owls, but, on balance, they should reconsider their habits because the advantages of being an early riser do not outweigh the productivity and leisure benefits associated with being active at night.

Ideally, travel should be fun, memorable, and exciting: a break from the mundane of everyday life. The human brain requires a healthy level of stimulation to trigger these feelings. While planning can provide order and peace of mind when traveling, it can also be what makes it predictable. Spontaneity is what unlocks it.

As stated by the late Anthony Bourdain, “I'm a big believer that you're never going to find [the] perfect city travel experience or the perfect meal without a constant willingness to experience a bad one.” In other words, there is a certain amount of risk and failure when it comes to meaningful travel. It is said that there are three types of fun: (1) fun that is fun in the moment, (2) fun that is fun upon reflection, and (3) fun that is pure regret. Playing the balance between the three types of fun categorizes Bourdain’s sentiment. Obviously, Type 3 fun should be avoided, which is easy with basic precautions and awareness. Type 1 fun is safe, like watching your favorite T.V. show or make dinner with friends. Making this a goal for travel allows travel to mimic everyday and not special. Instead, Type 2 fun is not enjoyable at the moment, but the adversities that make it such makes it story-worthy and memorable. Traveling without concrete plans forces exploration and improvisation, creating more opportunities for Type 2 fun.

Granted there are always elements of a trip that need to be planned: scheduling time off, transportation, packing. Planning can plant expectations, which may make the trip predictable. Oftentimes the research involved relies on reading blogs, travel guides, and content from someone who has made judgements about destinations. Their goal is to find the experiences that will trigger Type 1 fun in most people. However, given that Type 2 fun is defined by an individual’s comfort level, these recommendations must prioritize agreeableness and recognition over uniqueness.

Given any area of technology, improvement is guaranteed given time. Communication for example has progressed from yelling super loud to being able to send information to anybody, anywhere in the world, instantaneously. Reading as a technology is no exception. From the first images painted in ochre on cave walls, to the printing press, to the book, and finally the e-reader. Brands such as Nook and Kindle have revolutionized the technology of reading and have improved the experience over the predecessor: paper pages.

The “biggest” advantage to reading books digitally is size and space. An enthusiast could fit a single smaller paperback novel into the back pocket of their jeans, for example. Anything larger requires to be carried by hand. Naturally the solution to this would be to carry large books in a backpack. While this affords more literature capacity, the backpack is still limited to five or six large books. A digital reader on the other hand is able to fit millions of books and documents into the space of a small book or magazine. One reader, tablet, or laptop can fit more information on it than can be found in entire buildings designed for storing books (i.e. libraries). The space factor is an indisputable advantage of digital reading over print reading.

Some would argue that an advantage to print reading is energy efficiency. If the digital reader device loses power, no more reading. While this is a reasonable argument, the ubiquity of charging cables and electrical access really only allows this to be an advantage if the reader somehow forgets to charge their reading device, AND does not have a charger, AND does not have access to an electrical outlet. Even in this situation, there is only a problem if the reader isn’t busy doing other things and was intending to sit down and read. In the event that all of the above is true, it may have been preferable to have a paper book. That being said, the reader still needs a light source to be able to see the book, which a digital reader will often have built in, negating the need to turn lights on. This essentially nullifies the energy efficiency argument.

While there are certainly other valid arguments such as the enjoyment of reading print on paper, those arguments are all subjective. As demonstrated above, digital reading is preferable to reading print on paper.

It’s not uncommon to hear “the early bird takes the worm” throughout your years, especially in your adolescent time. Time and time again, it proves a point, being a morning person is better than being a night owl.

Seeing through the lens of a young adult, initially when I heard it’s better to be a morning person, I refused to agree. But over the years, I have understood the many benefits. The first benefit that morning people have is that they are more productive in the day. If they have work that is during the typical work hours (9am - 5pm), they would be able to fully functional and be able to converse with their coworkers during the same hours. Here’s an example to elaborate: I had a job that had asynchronous hours. I worked at night because I wasn’t a morning person and my sleep schedule was messed up. The issue I found was that when I would work at 8pm and had a question for my coworker, I would be unable to get a fast response from them. Of course there was no expectations to hear from them at that hour, but when I would wake up early and work during normal work hours, I would get a faster response. So if my sleep schedule was better and I can wake up early, my work can be more productive.

Another reason why it’s better is you can have more sunlight if you are a morning person. If you mentally do better with sunlight, then you should heavily consider being a morning person. You wake up to sunlight and can go to sleep when there’s no more sun as per societal expectations are. You’re maximizing the amount of life.

Lastly, there have been multiple studies done that whoever is a morning person has a higher amount of energy compared to people who aren’t morning people. It’s been reported morning people have faster reaction times and higher concentration times.

There is an old saying that my grandma used to tell me, that the early bird catches the worm. When she used to say this, it was about getting me to get up early to brush my teeth as a 10-year-old boy. Little did I know that this went from being a quirk to defining my personality long after in my adult life, yielding so much advantage in productivity, health, and overall well-being.

Thinking about it, the day starts in the morning, so it is only natural to see that morning people are naturally aligned with the rhythm of the world. Business hours, school schedules, and medical appointments are all planned around daylight hours, mostly at the start of the morning. Those who wake early are thus better positioned to move through the current seamlessly rather than against it, and that sense of less friction makes both professional and personal lives easier. The nighthawk, on the other hand, would perpetually battle against that natural rhythm.

Beyond the ease that rising early brings, the psychological benefits are well-documented. The silence that one gets to experience before the many noises of the day allows one to engage with important activities such as exercise, journaling, and, even more importantly, planning the day. That proactive start cultivates a sense of control and, even more critically, intentionality that powers you through the day. No wonder early risers experience higher levels of positivity, lower rates of feeling down, and a general connection with community compared to late-night individuals.

The night person is less likely to engage in activities that happen during the day, such as exercising in the gym, purchasing fresh food from the stores, or even enjoying a show in the cinema. Being able to maintain a sleep schedule that matches the societal expectation even makes the rest that early risers experience more wholesome.

While the advantages stack up for early risers, that is not to say that night owls are doomed or lack discipline. But then, in a world that rewards patterns, structure, and alignment, the morning person simply has more working in their favor. It pays better to rise with the sun.

Reading books in print is preferable to reading them digitally for many people, who cite the comforting weight of a hardcover volume in their hands or the distinctive scent associated with old books among their reasons they feel this way. Simply put, there is something better, aesthetically speaking, about printed books. Intuitively, there seems to be something to this: any number of other activities we do for enjoyment seem to be enhanced by engaging multiple modes of sensory input, from the music played in art galleries to the pine-scented candles many burn during their Christmas celebrations to “get into the spirit.” There is nothing obviously different about reading for pleasure. Indeed, many feel that reading is enhanced by sipping a hot beverage or being sat in their favorite chair.

Print books are preferable even when reading for work or study. Printing provides an unintentional benefit in the form of margin space which can be used for writing one’s own notes or comments. While many digital reading softwares now provide the option to annotate while one reads, what is less common is the ability to do so with handwriting rather than typing. Since at least 2009, scholars of psychology and cognitive science have been learning that handwritten notes offer their writer better recall of the material they contain versus type-written notes, perhaps even if the material is never reviewed. While these digital softwares could be adjusted to include this feature, at the time of this writing, this is a very uncommon feature.

Proponents of digital reading softwares and devices are quick to point out that they are space-saving and often more cost effective, but in so doing they miss these core values for those who prefer printed books. Printed books require space to store them, usually on shelves, but can also be used aesthetically to use the objects in one’s home or office to reflect their inhabitants’ interests. A digital book can be annotated, but one cannot wrestle with the text’s arguments barehanded in their quest to understand and enjoy. What is aesthetically better about print books than digital, then, is that they are more personal. For those reading not just to learn, but to connect with the text—and through it, with other people—this will likely always be preferable.

Digital reading has transformed the way people access and engage with books, and for good reason. While many readers hold a nostalgic attachment to physical books, reading digitally is simply the superior choice for modern students and lifelong learners alike. The benefits of digital reading — in terms of convenience, accessibility, and functionality — far outweigh any advantages that print can offer.

The most obvious advantage of digital reading is convenience. A single e-reader or tablet can store thousands of books, meaning a student no longer has to lug heavy textbooks across campus or worry about leaving a book at home. Traveling with an entire personal library in your pocket is something print books could never offer. For a college student managing multiple classes and commuting between locations, this kind of portability is genuinely life-changing.

Beyond convenience, digital books dramatically improve accessibility. Many platforms offer adjustable font sizes, background colors, and text-to-speech features that make reading easier for people with dyslexia, visual impairments, or other learning differences. Print books simply cannot accommodate these needs without specialized editions that are often expensive and hard to obtain. Digital reading levels the playing field and ensures more people can engage meaningfully with literature and educational material.

Digital reading also enhances the actual experience of engaging with a text. Built-in dictionaries allow readers to look up unfamiliar words instantly without losing their place, and highlighting and note-taking features make studying far more organized and efficient. Many apps even sync notes across devices, so annotations made on a phone can be reviewed on a laptop later. Anyone who has tried to manage messy handwritten margin notes in a print textbook will immediately appreciate how much cleaner and more practical digital annotation is.

Critics often argue that reading on screens causes eye strain and reduces comprehension, but these concerns are increasingly outdated as screen technology improves. Modern e-ink displays, like those found on Kindles, are designed specifically to mimic the appearance of paper and reduce glare significantly. With proper screen settings and reading habits, these issues are easily managed.

Digital reading is not simply a trend — it is a meaningful improvement over print that makes books more accessible, more portable, and more interactive for today's readers.

There is something irreplaceable about holding a physical book in your hands, and despite the convenience of tablets and e-readers, print books remain the superior way to read. From improved comprehension to the simple pleasure of the experience itself, reading in print offers advantages that digital formats simply cannot match.

One of the strongest arguments for print reading is its positive effect on comprehension and retention. Multiple studies, including research published in the journal Reading and Writing, have found that readers who use printed text tend to understand and remember material more deeply than those reading the same content on screens. This is partly because physical books encourage a more linear reading experience. Readers are less tempted to skim or jump around the way they naturally do on digital devices, where hyperlinks and notifications constantly compete for attention. When a college student sits down with a printed textbook or novel, the focus stays on the page.

Beyond comprehension, print books also protect physical health in ways that matter. Extended screen time contributes to digital eye strain, headaches, and disrupted sleep patterns. The blue light emitted by electronic screens has been shown to interfere with melatonin production, making it harder to fall asleep after an evening of reading on a device. A printed book, by contrast, requires no backlight and produces no blue light, making it a healthier choice for nighttime reading. Students already spend enormous amounts of time staring at screens for classes, social media, and entertainment, so choosing print for leisure or study reading provides a meaningful break.

There is also a practical and emotional dimension to owning physical books. Annotating a print book with handwritten notes, underlining passages, and folding corners creates a personal relationship with the text that digital highlighting simply does not replicate. Many readers feel a genuine sense of accomplishment when they finish a book they can place on a shelf. That physical object becomes a record of intellectual growth and time spent.

While digital reading certainly has its place for convenience and portability, print books offer deeper comprehension, better health outcomes, and a richer personal experience. For serious readers who want to truly engage with a text, the printed page remains the gold standard.

In an era dominated by screens, the transition from physical books to digital e-readers is often framed as an inevitable progression toward efficiency. However, the experience of reading a printed book remains fundamentally superior to digital reading due to its impact on cognitive retention, tactile engagement, and the preservation of mental well-being.

One of the most compelling arguments for print is its effect on focus and comprehension. Digital devices are designed for multitasking, often bombarding the reader with notifications or the temptation to browse the internet. Research suggests that reading on a screen encourages "skimming" rather than "deep reading." In contrast, a physical book provides a distraction-free environment. Without the blue light of a tablet or the scroll of a smartphone, the brain can enter a state of deep immersion. Furthermore, the spatial memory associated with physical pages helps with information retention; being able to visualize exactly where a passage was located on a specific page helps the mind map information more effectively than an endless digital scroll.

Beyond the cognitive benefits, the tactile nature of print creates a more meaningful connection to the material. There is a psychological satisfaction in physically turning pages and seeing the progress made through the thickness of the book. Digital files are ethereal and easily forgotten, but a personal library serves as a physical record of one’s intellectual journey. Books are not just data containers; they are artifacts. They can be annotated in the margins, gifted to friends, or kept for decades without worrying about file compatibility or battery life.

Finally, print media offers a necessary respite from "digital fatigue." Most students and professionals spend their entire day staring at monitors. Choosing a physical book for leisure or study allows the eyes to rest and helps regulate circadian rhythms, particularly during nighttime reading. By choosing print, readers reclaim their attention from the algorithms and notifications that govern modern life. While e-readers offer convenience, they cannot replicate the focus, permanence, and sensory satisfaction of a printed volume. For anyone seeking to truly absorb knowledge and enjoy a quiet moment of reflection, the physical book remains the superior medium.

While the romanticized image of a spontaneous traveler wandering through foreign streets without a plan appeals to many, the reality of travel often involves logistical hurdles that can ruin a trip if not handled in advance. Planning a trip in detail is ultimately better than traveling spontaneously because it ensures time efficiency, financial stability, and a higher quality of overall experience. Without a roadmap, travelers often find themselves wasting their limited vacation days on mundane tasks rather than enjoying their destination.

The primary advantage of detailed planning is the optimization of time. When a traveler arrives in a new city with a firm itinerary, they avoid the "decision paralysis" that plagues spontaneous tourists. Instead of spending hours in a hotel lobby researching train schedules or checking museum opening times, a prepared traveler can move seamlessly from one activity to the next. For example, popular attractions like the Uffizi Gallery in Florence or the Alhambra in Spain often require tickets to be booked months in advance. A spontaneous traveler would likely arrive only to find these sites sold out, missing the very landmarks they traveled across the world to see.

Furthermore, planning provides a necessary financial safety net. Last-minute travel is almost always more expensive. Booking flights, accommodation, and transportation months ahead of time allows travelers to lock in lower rates and budget accurately. Spontaneity often leads to "desperation spending," where a traveler is forced to pay exorbitant prices for a mediocre hotel room or a last-minute taxi because they didn't research public transit or book a room during peak season. By planning, one can allocate funds toward high-quality dining or unique experiences rather than wasting money on avoidable logistical emergencies.

Finally, a detailed plan actually reduces stress, allowing for deeper relaxation. The anxiety of not knowing where one will sleep or how to get to the next destination can overshadow the joy of the journey. When the logistics are settled, the mind is free to fully engage with the local culture. Detailed planning does not mean every minute is scripted; rather, it provides a solid foundation that allows for meaningful exploration without the looming threat of travel mishaps. Ultimately, preparation is the key to a successful and fulfilling journey.

Planning a trip in detail is better than traveling spontaneously because it saves time, reduces stress, and makes the overall experience more meaningful. While spontaneous travel may sound exciting, careful planning usually leads to a smoother and more enjoyable trip, especially when time and money are limited.

One major advantage of detailed planning is that it helps travelers use their time wisely. Vacations are often short, and without a plan, people can waste hours deciding where to go, how to get there, or what to do next. If someone visits a city like Rome without researching in advance, they might miss important places such as the Colosseum or the Vatican simply because they did not realize tickets should be booked early. A good itinerary allows travelers to organize their days efficiently and include the attractions that matter most to them.

Planning also reduces unnecessary stress. Travel already includes enough uncertainty with delayed flights, unfamiliar streets, and language barriers. Having hotel reservations, transportation schedules, and a list of activities prepared in advance creates a sense of security. Instead of worrying about where to sleep or whether a train is sold out, travelers can focus on enjoying the destination. This is especially important for families, students, or anyone traveling on a budget, since last-minute choices are often more expensive and less convenient. Booking flights and hotels early can save a significant amount of money, which leaves more room for enjoyable experiences.

In addition, detailed planning does not remove fun; it actually makes fun more likely. When people prepare, they can learn about local customs, food, and special events before they arrive. For example, someone going to Japan during cherry blossom season can research the best parks and dates for viewing them, creating memories that might be missed on a purely spontaneous trip. Planning can still leave room for flexibility, such as free time in the afternoon or a chance to explore a neighborhood unexpectedly. The difference is that the most important parts of the trip are protected.

For all of these reasons, planning a trip in detail is better than traveling spontaneously. It helps travelers make better use of their time, lowers stress, saves money, and increases the chance of having unforgettable experiences. A well-planned trip offers both structure and enjoyment, which makes travel more rewarding in the end.

Traveling spontaneously is one of the most rewarding ways to explore the world, and while detailed planning might seem safer, it often strips away the very qualities that make travel exciting. Spontaneous travel allows for genuine discovery, personal growth, and memorable experiences that no itinerary could ever schedule.

One of the strongest arguments for spontaneous travel is the freedom it creates. When you are not locked into hotel reservations, timed tours, or rigid daily schedules, you can follow your curiosity wherever it leads. Imagine arriving in a new city and stumbling upon a local street festival that would have been missed entirely if you had been rushing to your pre-booked museum tour. These unplanned moments often become the highlight of a trip precisely because they feel authentic and unforced. A traveler who leaves room for the unexpected is far more likely to have a story worth telling.

Spontaneous travel also encourages personal adaptability and confidence. When you do not have every detail mapped out, you are forced to problem-solve in real time, talk to strangers for directions or recommendations, and make decisions independently. These challenges build resilience and self-reliance in ways that following a step-by-step itinerary simply cannot. Many experienced travelers describe their most transformative trips as the ones where something went "wrong" and they had to navigate the situation creatively. Those moments of improvisation often lead to the richest cultural exchanges and the deepest connections with locals.

Critics of spontaneous travel argue that planning prevents wasted time and saves money through advance bookings. This is a fair point, but it overlooks the value of flexibility. Yes, last-minute accommodations might occasionally cost more, but the experiences gained by staying in an unexpected town or extending your time in a city you fell in love with far outweigh the financial difference. Additionally, over-planning can create anxiety when things deviate from the schedule, which they almost always do.

Ultimately, travel is about experiencing life beyond your usual boundaries. Detailed plans can actually limit that experience by prioritizing efficiency over exploration. Spontaneous travel trusts the journey itself, and more often than not, that trust is rewarded with something truly extraordinary.

Traveling spontaneously is better than planning a trip in detail because it makes the experience more exciting, more personal, and often less stressful. While careful planning can seem responsible, it can also turn a vacation into a strict schedule. A spontaneous trip allows people to enjoy the moment, follow their curiosity, and discover places they might never have chosen in advance.

One reason spontaneous travel is better is that it creates a stronger sense of adventure. When every hour of a trip is planned, there is little room for surprise. In contrast, traveling without a detailed schedule means each day can lead somewhere unexpected. A person might walk down a side street, hear music coming from a small café, and end up having the best night of the trip there. These unplanned moments often become the most memorable because they feel authentic rather than arranged. Instead of checking off a list of famous attractions, travelers can experience a place in a way that feels natural and alive.

Spontaneous travel also gives people more freedom to make choices based on how they feel in the moment. For example, if someone arrives in a city and loves it, they can stay longer. If they find another place disappointing, they can leave early and try somewhere else. Detailed plans can trap people into spending time and money on activities they no longer want to do. Flexibility is especially valuable when traveling because energy, weather, and local recommendations can all change quickly. A rainy day might ruin a planned outdoor tour, but a spontaneous traveler can easily shift plans and discover a museum, bookstore, or restaurant instead.

Finally, traveling spontaneously can reduce pressure. Detailed planning often creates high expectations, and when one reservation fails or a schedule falls apart, the trip can feel ruined. People may become more focused on managing time than actually enjoying themselves. Spontaneous travel encourages a more relaxed attitude. It reminds travelers that the purpose of a trip is not to control every detail but to explore, learn, and have fun.

For these reasons, spontaneous travel is preferable to detailed trip planning. It brings adventure, flexibility, and peace of mind. The best journeys are often the ones that leave room for the unexpected.

Being a Morning Person Is the Better Way to Live

There is an ongoing debate about whether it is better to be a morning person or a night owl, and while both lifestyles have their defenders, rising early is clearly the more beneficial approach. Morning people consistently enjoy advantages in productivity, health, and social alignment that night owls simply cannot match.

One of the strongest arguments for being a morning person is the productivity boost that comes with starting the day early. The early morning hours are typically free from distractions. There are no phone notifications flooding in, no social obligations pulling at your attention, and no background noise from a busy world. Students and professionals who wake up early often report completing their most demanding work before others have even had their first cup of coffee. For example, a student who wakes at six in the morning can finish a difficult assignment before class, leaving the rest of the day open for other responsibilities rather than scrambling at midnight.

Beyond productivity, the physical health benefits of a morning schedule are well documented. Waking up early tends to align with natural sunlight cycles, which helps regulate the body's circadian rhythm. This alignment improves sleep quality, stabilizes mood, and even supports a stronger immune system. Night owls, by contrast, often find themselves sleeping through daylight hours or staying awake long past when their bodies want to rest, which research has linked to higher risks of depression and metabolic issues.

There is also the practical matter of fitting into society's structure. The world is built around morning schedules. Schools start early, most jobs begin in the morning, and important appointments are typically scheduled during daytime hours. A night owl constantly fights against this structure, arriving tired to early commitments and feeling perpetually out of sync. Morning people move through these demands with ease because their natural rhythm matches what the world actually requires.

Ultimately, being a morning person is not just a personal preference but a genuine advantage. The quiet focus of early hours, the health benefits of a regulated sleep schedule, and the simple ease of functioning within society's timetable all make rising early the smarter and more sustainable way to live.

Society often romanticizes the "night owl" lifestyle as the hallmark of the creative or the driven, but in reality, being a morning person provides a significant advantage in terms of health, productivity, and social alignment. While staying up late might feel like reclaiming personal time, waking up early allows an individual to operate in harmony with the natural world and the structures of modern life.

The primary benefit of being a morning person is the boost in cognitive performance and productivity. Studies consistently show that proactive behavior and better grades are linked to early risers. When you wake up before the rest of the world, you gain a period of uninterrupted focus. There are no pings from work emails, no social media distractions, and no household noise. This "golden hour" allows for deep work or exercise, setting a positive tone for the rest of the day. By the time most people are starting their first cup of coffee, a morning person has already accomplished their most difficult tasks, leading to reduced stress levels as the afternoon approaches.

Furthermore, being a morning person aligns perfectly with the "social clock." The professional world, educational institutions, and government services all operate on a 9-to-5 schedule. Night owls often suffer from "social jetlag," a state where their internal biological clock is at odds with their external obligations. This constant friction leads to chronic sleep deprivation and decreased mental clarity. In contrast, morning people are most alert when the world demands it of them. They do not have to fight their biology to be present at a morning meeting or a 10:00 a.m. lecture.

Finally, there are undeniable health benefits. Early risers tend to have more consistent sleep patterns, which regulates hormones and improves mood. Exposure to early morning sunlight helps stabilize the circadian rhythm and boosts Vitamin D and serotonin levels. This natural regulation makes it easier to maintain a healthy diet and a regular fitness routine, as people are less likely to skip a workout due to the exhaustion that accumulates by the end of a long day. Ultimately, the discipline of a morning routine fosters a sense of control and well-being that late-night habits simply cannot match.

Modern society often glorifies the "early bird," equating sunrise productivity with moral superiority and professional success. However, the traditional nine-to-five schedule is increasingly outdated in a globalized, digital world. Being a night owl is actually the superior lifestyle because it fosters a unique environment for deep focus, creative breakthroughs, and a richer social life that the rigid morning routine cannot provide.

The primary advantage of being a night owl is the profound silence and lack of distraction that occurs after the rest of the world goes to sleep. During the day, constant notifications, emails, and social obligations fragment our attention. For a morning person, the peak of their day is often interrupted by the noise of a functioning society. In contrast, the hours between midnight and 4:00 AM offer a "flow state" that is nearly impossible to achieve during daylight. In this window, the pressure of immediate response vanishes, allowing for deep work on complex projects, such as coding, writing, or studying. Many of history’s greatest innovators have credited their late-night vigils for their most creative insights, as the brain tends to make more unconventional connections when the analytical constraints of the "business day" are lifted.

Furthermore, being a late riser aligns better with the rhythm of modern social and cultural life. Most meaningful human connections—concerts, dinners, theater, and deep conversations among friends—happen in the evening. A morning person is often forced to choose between their strict sleep schedule and these vital experiences, frequently leaving events early or suffering from exhaustion the next day. A night owl, however, is at their cognitive and social peak when the world is most vibrant. They do not have to sacrifice their social well-being to maintain their productivity.

While critics argue that late sleepers are "lazy," this is a fundamental misunderstanding of biological chronotypes. Being a night owl is about shifting one's peak performance to a time of day that is inherently more peaceful and conducive to original thought. By embracing the night, individuals can claim a sense of autonomy over their time, trading the frantic energy of the morning for the creative sanctuary of the dark.

Being a night owl is better than being a morning person because nighttime offers a kind of freedom, focus, and creativity that is hard to find earlier in the day. While morning people often get praised for waking up early, staying up late can be just as productive, and in many situations it is even more enjoyable and useful. For students, artists, and anyone who needs uninterrupted time to think, the night can be the best part of the day.

One reason being a night owl is preferable is that the world becomes quieter at night. During the day, there are constant distractions such as phone notifications, errands, family activity, and social expectations. At night, many of those interruptions disappear. This makes it easier to concentrate on homework, reading, or creative projects. For example, many college students find that they write their best essays late at night because they can finally sit down without being interrupted every few minutes. The calm atmosphere helps people think more deeply and work more efficiently.

Another advantage is that nighttime often encourages creativity. In the late hours, people are not rushing from one responsibility to another. Instead, they have space to reflect and let their thoughts develop. Writers, musicians, and designers often describe nighttime as the moment when their best ideas appear. There is something about the quiet and privacy of the night that allows imagination to grow. Even students who are not artists may find that they solve problems more easily or come up with stronger ideas for class discussions when working at night.

Being a night owl can also fit modern life better than people admit. Many jobs, classes, and responsibilities now happen online, which gives people more flexibility in choosing when they do their work. Not everyone has the same natural body clock, so it makes sense to value a schedule that matches how a person actually functions best. For some people, forcing themselves to be cheerful and alert at 6:00 a.m. only leads to stress and poor performance, while staying up later feels natural and productive.

In the end, being a night owl is better because it offers peace, stronger focus, and more room for creativity. Rather than treating early risers as automatically more responsible, society should recognize that late-night thinkers often do their best work when everyone else is asleep.
